Moderate populist people-centrism (59.68) and low political efficacy (20.08) support easier registration, but libertarian skepticism of state control (45.71) creates some resistance.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low openness to experience and preference for national unity create mild opposition to proportional representation as potentially fragmenting.

High openness to experience (80.0) and participatory democracy preference (14.41) lean toward proportional representation, but very high system justification (96.05) and strong national order preference (92.02) create resistance to major electoral system changes.
High openness to experience (82.34) and populist people-centrism (59.68) favor proportional representation, but low political efficacy (20.08) creates some skepticism about implementation.
Slight opposition due to low openness to new systems and authoritarian preference for stable majorities, though populist anti-elitism provides some counterweight.

Low GAL-TAN participatory democracy (28.17) and high authoritarianism (94.28) favor majoritarian systems for stability, but high populist people-centrism (88.02) creates mild openness to proportionality, resulting in near-neutrality.

High anti-elitism (84.28) and low trust in parliament (6.01) support proportional representation as a reform, but high collective narcissism (61.75) and ingroup loyalty (98.81) create some tension, resulting in moderate support.

Low GAL-TAN green-libertarian (3.84%) and high authoritarian submission (71.55%) oppose proportional representation as it fragments authority, while high conventionalism (84.84%) favors majoritarian systems.

High authoritarianism and a preference for strong, decisive government, combined with a belief that proportional systems can lead to fragmentation and instability, produce clear opposition to electoral reform toward proportionality as it is seen as a threat to effective governance and clear majorities.
